coredata.dc:title: Evolution of large stokes shift and non-radiative energy transfer phenomenon in sustainable blue-fluorescent CQDs upon subnanomolar detection of Acebrophylline

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as powerful fluorescent sensors for identifying harmful compounds in environmental and biological samples, due to their robust and adjustable emission characteristics. In this study, we explore CQDs (size ∼ 3 nm), as a probe, derived from Walnut Shell (WS) biomass waste for detecting Acebrophylline (AB), a respiratory disease medicine. From the selectivity studies, the calculated energy transfer between the CQDs (10 mM; donor) and AB (10 mM; acceptor) was found to be 64 %, attributed to the formation of a ground state complex, CQDs + AB. The CQDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity to AB in concentrations between 1–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.142 nM (R2 = 0.991, Ka = 1.39194 × 10−3 M−1). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments validated the static quenching of CQDs (3.46 → 3.71 ns) when exposed to AB. The proposed detection method was successfully applied for detecting AB in human urine samples with a good recovery percentage (81 to 123 %; RSD ca. 1 %). After AB sensing, changes in the CQDs’ crystalline nature, elemental composition, and chemical state were examined using XRD, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy. Microscopy imaging techniques (FESEM, HRTEM, and AFM) confirmed morphological changes of CQDs from spherical to agglomerated with an average diameter of approximately 14 nm. Additionally, the impact of time, pH, and interferons on AB sensing was investigated. In vitro anti-inflammatory activity and in vivo bioimaging studies on zebrafish were also performed. This study highlights several advantages, including a cost-effective and eco-friendly approach for healthcare applications.

head.ce:title: Evolution of large stokes shift and non-radiative energy transfer phenomenon in sustainable blue-fluorescent CQDs upon subnanomolar detection of Acebrophylline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as powerful fluorescent sensors for identifying harmful compounds in environmental and biological samples, due to their robust and adjustable emission characteristics. In this study, we explore CQDs (size ∼ 3 nm), as a probe, derived from Walnut Shell (WS) biomass waste for detecting Acebrophylline (AB), a respiratory disease medicine. From the selectivity studies, the calculated energy transfer between the CQDs (10 mM; donor) and AB (10 mM; acceptor) was found to be 64 %, attributed to the formation of a ground state complex, CQDs + AB. The CQDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity to AB in concentrations between 1–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.142 nM (R2 = 0.991, Ka = 1.39194 × 10−3 M−1). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments validated the static quenching of CQDs (3.46 → 3.71 ns) when exposed to AB. The proposed detection method was successfully applied for detecting AB in human urine samples with a good recovery percentage (81 to 123 %; RSD ca. 1 %). After AB sensing, changes in the CQDs’ crystalline nature, elemental composition, and chemical state were examined using XRD, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy. Microscopy imaging techniques (FESEM, HRTEM, and AFM) confirmed morphological changes of CQDs from spherical to agglomerated with an average diameter of approximately 14 nm. Additionally, the impact of time, pH, and interferons on AB sensing was investigated. In vitro anti-inflammatory activity and in vivo bioimaging studies on zebrafish were also performed. This study highlights several advantages, including a cost-effective and eco-friendly approach for healthcare applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors are essential for biological and environmental system monitoring because they provide precise, real-time analyte detection [1,2]. These are essential in healthcare for the diagnosis and treatment of chronic illnesses, especially respiratory ailments, which are among the world's leading causes of death. The World Health Organization (WHO) reports that respiratory conditions such as bronchial asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) are among the top ten causes of death worldwide [3–5]. Chronic airway inflammation, which varies greatly from person to person, is a characteristic of several disorders [6]. Monitoring therapeutic medications such as Acebrophylline (AB), an anti-inflammatory xanthine derivative used to treat asthma and COPD, is frequently necessary for the effective treatment of these conditions [7]. AB (Scheme 1 ) a drug composed of ambroxol and theophylline-7-acetate, exhibits anti-inflammatory properties with a chemical composition of theophylline-7-acetate in a stoichiometric ratio of 38.7 % acid to 61.3 % base, bonded with the amino group of ambroxol [8]. The permissible AB dosage levels are 28.0 mg/kg/day for children and 100 mg/kg/day for adults [9]. Despite its medicinal advantages, AB can have negative side effects including gastrointestinal problems, skin rashes, and renal failure, therefore careful dosage monitoring is necessary for everyone's health and safety [10–12].

Numerous analytical methods have previously been utilized for the quantification of AB, including UV–vis spectrophotometry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS), and thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [13–16]. These techniques have disadvantages such as lengthy sample preparation and complex procedural requirements [17,18]. In contrast, fluorescence spectrophotometry offers a rapid, sensitive, and selective determination of analytes without the need for complicated sample preparation or sophisticated instrumentation [19,20]. This necessitates the design of a selective material with the appropriate optical properties for the fluorescence detection of AB.

The outstanding optical characteristics of conventional fluorescence sensors based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are well recognized. However, their toxicity, chemical inertness, and low water stability make it difficult to use them in biological applications [21–23]. In contrast, because of their excellent fluorescence characteristics, environmental safety, and biocompatibility, carbon-based nanostructures like graphene oxide (GO) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown a great deal of promise for biosensing applications. For example, theophylline, a medication associated with AB, was detected at the nanomolar level using a GO-based fluorescence sensor, with a detection limit of 4 nM and a linear range from 10 to 300 nM [24]. As next-generation fluorescence sensors, CQDs have lately gained popularity due to their superior luminous qualities and high quantum yields [25]. Biosynthesized CQDs from natural and waste-derived sources offer a sustainable and safer alternative to the frequently used precursors in CQD synthesis, such as sucrose, citric acid, or chitosan, which can present toxicity and environmental problems [26–28]. Recent research has focused on the biosynthesis of CQDs from natural sources that are non-toxic, biodegradable, chemically inert, and environmentally safe [29].

In the past, our research team has successfully synthesized CQDs from biomass waste materials, including WS, rice husks [30], bamboo stems [31], and groundnut and pistachio shells [32] to detect hazardous materials. Previously, WS-derived CQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and applied for the detection of the insecticide imidacloprid [33]. The development of affordable, sensitive, and sustainable sensing technologies for respiratory treatments has advanced significantly with this work, which is the first report on the nanomolar detection of AB utilizing CQD-based fluorescence sensors. This report focuses on the fluorescence detection of AB at sub-nanomolar concentrations using CQDs derived from WS biomass waste. The selectivity and sensitivity of the as-prepared CQDs towards AB, as well as the effects of time, pH, and potential interferences, are evaluated. Additionally, the changes in morphology, structure, and optical properties of the CQDs post-AB sensing are examined. The proposed mechanism and mode of interaction between AB and CQDs are detailed. Furthermore, the in vitro anti-inflammatory activity of the biomass-derived CQDs + AB complex and in vivo bioimaging of CQDs in zebrafish are discussed. This study demonstrates a sustainable approach to converting waste materials into valuable resources.

CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal technique. Walnuts were initially obtained from a local store, and shells were then broken down. The shells were washed using deionized (DI) water, sun-dried for 24 h, and then roasted at 200 ℃. The initial brownish-colored WS turns black and is then meshed to form a fine black powder. 0.72 g of fine WS black powder was dissolved in 36 mL of DI water and stirred for 30 min at 40 °C to produce a homogeneous solution. This homogenous solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in the oven for 3 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 µm PVDF membrane. Finally, the yellow solution containing CQDs was collected and analyzed for further study [33].

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.56 Å). Surface morphology was examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) on a Carl Zeiss Sigma 300 detector at Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Erode, India. Raman spectra were obtained with an Alpha 300RA spectrometer at the Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), Mahatma Gandhi University (MGU), Kottayam, India. AFM images were captured using a WiTec alpha 300 instrument, Germany, at the PSG Institute of Advanced Studies (PSGIAS), Coimbatore, India. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a SHIMADZU IRTRACER 100 at SRM University, Chennai, India. HRTEM analysis was carried out using a Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN at Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. XPS measurements were conducted using a K-Alpha-KAN9954133 instrument at Pondicherry University, Pondicherry, India. Absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrometer. Fluorescence analysis was performed using a Jasco FP-8250 spectrofluorometer. Time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements were conducted using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique, with IBH Data Station software V2.6 (Horiba – Deltaflax) at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. Anti-inflammatory activity was analyzed using an ELISA plate analyzer (ROBONIK) at the University of Madras, Chennai, India. Bioimaging studies were performed under an inverted microscope at 4x magnification (EVOS FLC, Life Technologies, USA) at the DRDO-Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the proposed sensing method, a real-time experiment was conducted using a human urine sample obtained from a healthy individual aged approximately twenty-five years. For the urine samples for analysis, we used a 0.22 μm syringe filter and centrifuged the samples for 10 min to remove debris and cells. The purified urine samples were then diluted with deionized water to make a volume of 10 mL. The known concentration of AB (3.3 µM) was spiked into the urine samples. Following this, the CQDs solution was added to each spiked sample, and fluorescence measurements were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The recovery percentage of AB from the real samples was calculated using Eq. (1).

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and lysozyme (Ly) (1 % aqueous solutions) were mixed with various concentrations of CQDs + AB (10, 30, and 50 µL). The pH of the solution was adjusted to approximately 6.3 by adding 1.0 N HCl. These samples were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min, followed by heating at 60 °C in a water bath for 30 min. After cooling to room temperature, the absorbance at 660 nm was measured using a spectrophotometer. Diclofenac sodium was used as the standard medication. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate, and the percentage inhibition of protein denaturation was calculated using Eq. (2) [34,35].

D. rerio is widely regarded as an ideal vertebrate model organism due to its transparent embryos, which allow for continuous monitoring of organogenesis [36]. In this study, adult D. rerio specimens were obtained from Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, and acclimatized according to OECD 2013 guidelines. Breeding was conducted using a 2:1 male-to-female ratio, and eggs were collected for embryotoxicity testing in accordance with OECD 236 guidelines. The experimental setup included a control group and four treatment groups with varying concentrations of CQDs (10–40 µM). A total of 50 embryos per group were individually exposed in 5-well beakers from 24 to 96 h post-fertilization (hpf). Observations were made at intervals from 24 to 96 hpf using an inverted microscope.

CQDs were synthesized following established literature procedures and characterized using various analytical techniques [33]. Several fluorescence quenching mechanisms, including fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), energy transfer (ET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE), have been reported. These processes do not require the formation of covalent bonds between the fluorophore and the quencher, making the quenching mechanism simple and adaptable compared to other methods [37]. In the present study, we focused on the energy transfer process to investigate the fluorescence emission quenching of CQDs upon the introduction of AB.

The fluorescence emission of CQDs was measured at 433 nm (λex = 360 nm). Selectivity and sensitivity experiments were conducted by monitoring the changes in CQD fluorescence emission upon the addition of 2 mL of an aqueous solution containing various respiratory drugs. The initial volume of CQDs was 2 mL, and excitation and emission measurements were taken at 360 nm and 433 nm, respectively. To evaluate the selective drug-sensing property of CQDs, various respiratory drugs (10 mM), including AB, Salbutamol (SB), Labetalol (LB), Theophylline (TP), and Levosalbutamol (LSB), were added to the CQDs solution. Firstly, the addition of AB (10 mM) resulted in a significant quenching (48 %) in the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs (100 mM), as shown in Fig. 1 . In contrast, when other respiratory drugs were added, the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs (100 mM) decreased slightly (26 % at 10 mM). However, when we reduced the concentration of CQDs to 10 mM (10 times less than the initial analysis) the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs was significantly quenched to 64 % only upon the addition of AB (10 mM) (Figure SI1). The addition of other respiratory drugs resulted in a slight decrease in CQD’s fluorescence emission intensity (approximately 35 % at 10 mM). These results confirmed the high selectivity of CQDs towards AB over the other examined respiratory drugs.

Based on the results of the selectivity study, the sensitivity of CQDs towards AB was assessed using various concentrations of AB in an aqueous solution of CQDs (Fig. 2 a). As the concentration of AB increased (1 → 100 μM), the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs at 433 nm (λex = 360 nm) was quenched, without any shift in the emission wavelength or alteration like the emission peak. This decrease in fluorescence intensity upon the addition of AB suggests the involvement of different quenching mechanisms, which may be either static or dynamic. This was further confirmed by analyzing the intensity ratio (F0-F)/F versus [Q] (Figure SI2a). Generally, quenching mechanisms are classified into two types: static quenching, which involves the formation of a ground-state complex, and dynamic quenching, which involves collisional deactivation [38]. The observed changes in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs as a function of AB concentration illustrate the sensitivity of CQDs to varying levels of the drug. The aforementioned mechanism was verified by the Stern–Volmer plot (eq. (3) [39]:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
Where, F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensities of the CQDs in the presence and absence of the drugs, respectively. KSV is a stern-Volmer constant, and Q is the quencher concentration. From this plot, the limit of detection (LoD) and limit of quantification (LoQ) was calculated to be 0.142 and 0.473 nM, respectively (R2 = 0.99089) and the attained binding constant is 1.39194 × 10-3 M−1 (Fig. 2b). The results exhibited linearity within the concentration range of 1–100 μM, confirming the static quenching mechanism. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs is attributed to the interactions between their surface functional groups and AB. This quenching mechanism likely arises from the strong interactions between oxygen-containing functional groups—such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups CQDs and AB. These interactions facilitate effective energy transfer between the donor (100 mM of CQDs) and acceptor (AB). The non-radiative energy transfer efficiency between the donor (CQDs) and acceptor (AB) was calculated using the following equation (Eq. (4) and was found to be 28 % (Figure SI3). Similarly, energy transfer mechanism was confirmed using a low concentration of CQDs (10 mM) and the achieved energy transfer is 64 % (Fig. 3 a).
(4)

F
D A
F D
where FD indicates the intensity of CQDs (donor) alone and FDA indicates the intensity of CQDs + AB (acceptor). The formation of a ground-state complex which leads to non-radiative emission, is confirmed by the absorption spectrum (Figure SI2c).

CQD fluorescence can be quenched via the inner filter effect (IFE) or energy transfer mechanisms like Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or electron transfer (ET). IFE is the attenuation of the emitted radiation by the quencher, whereas FRET/ET is the transfer of energy from a donor to an acceptor [40]. In the current study, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of AB coincides considerably with the emission spectrum of CQDs, indicating energy transfer (Fig. 3a). This spectral overlap, represented by a Stokes shift, describes the energy difference between the emission and absorption spectra [41,42].

A large Stokes shift of 86 nm was revealed between the absorption peak of AB (274 nm) and the excitation peak of CQDs (360 nm), which reduced the self-quenching of CQDs and increased sensitivity. The decrease in spectrum overlap between donor excitation and acceptor absorption reduces self-absorption effects, which are frequent with small Stokes shifts. This overlap provides efficient energy transfer, as indicated by the emission of AB at 426 nm following CQD excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 3b), and the formation of a ground-state complex (CQDs + AB) is confirmed [43]. Noteworthy to mention that the large Stokes shifts are desirable for biological sensing because they limit self-absorption and improve assay sensitivity. In this regard, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs showed a strong linear correlation with the AB concentration (1 to 100 µM) (Figure SI2a), indicating their suitability for sensitive detection applications.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism, the UV–visible absorption wavelengths of CQDs, AB, and CQDs + AB were measured individually, yielding values of 278 nm, 275 nm, and 278 nm, respectively (Figure SI2c). Notably, the absorption peak observed at 278 nm (π → π*) upon mixing CQDs with AB does not align with the absorption peak of bare AB (275 nm) [44]. This discrepancy is attributed to the strong interaction between CQDs and AB, which may induce a fluorescence quenching effect by impeding the radiative recombination of excitons. Consequently, this quenching effect resulted in energy transfer (28 %) (Figure SI3) and the formation of a complex structure, (CQDs + AB) [45]. Furthermore, analysis of the HRTEM image revealed an increase in the particle size of CQDs from 3 to 14 nm, confirming the formation of CQDs + AB due to agglomeration, thereby facilitating the energy transfer quenching effect. Moreover, the size of the CQDs is about 3 nm, but the size of the CQDs + AB is about 14 nm, which indicates that the addition of AB may also lead to agglomeration of CQDs. For this agglomeration, a combined mechanism may be responsible for the PL quenching. Additionally, a schematic illustration mechanism of CQDs (donor) binding with AB (acceptor) to form a structure is provided (Scheme 2 ).

Energy is transferred between the excited states of CQDs and AB via interactions at specific places such as surface states, electronic states, and molecular structure proximity. The surface of CQDs, which is generally filled with functional groups or defects, plays an essential role in allowing energy exchanges with the AB. Effective energy transfer necessitates a close between CQDs and AB, allowing efficient pairing of their electronic or vibrational states to determine the interaction [46].

When CQDs are exposed to UV light (λex = 360 nm), electrons are excited from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (S1). Radiative recombination occurs in the CQDs system as part of the excited electrons in S1 return to S0, producing blue fluorescence. However, when AB is added to the CQDs system, electrons in S1 are transported to the AB rather than producing photons via a radiative pathway [45]. These findings indicate that the complexation and energy transfer between AB and CQDs causes PL quenching.

These transitions are seen in many sensors without any corresponding color change. This implies that the observed color shift may be caused by other mechanisms, such as the charge transfer mechanism. Electronic transitions like π → π* and n → π* are responsible for the color change in the CQDs that was observed in this investigation [47]. Even though these transitions are frequent in conjugated π-bonds and can modify optical properties at longer wavelengths, it doesn’t always produce appreciable color shifts [48]. This demonstrates how charge transfer mechanisms may contribute to the explanation of the observed phenomenon.

Charge transfer mechanism, which includes the rearrangement of electron density within a molecule or between interacting entities such as donor–acceptor pairs. These transitions frequently produce large absorption bands in the UV–vis spectrum, causing noticeable color changes. Charge transfer transitions cause larger shifts in absorption spectra than π → π* and n → π* transitions, indicating significant changes in electronic structure. The presence of donor–acceptor pairs, conjugated or heteroatoms with varied electron affinities lends validity to charge transfer as the fundamental mechanism behind the colorimetric response [49].

The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs in the presence and absence of the quencher AB was recorded (Fig. 4 ). Initially, the fluorescence lifetime of bare CQDs was best fitted with a tri-exponential function, yielding an average lifetime of 3.46 ns (Table 1 ). The fitted tri-exponential function (eq. (5) suggests that fluorescence lifetime occurs through a three-way relaxation path.

where, “τ” is a fluorescence lifetime, and “A” represents the amplitude of the corresponding lifetime. The addition of AB (0.25 eq.) slightly increases the average lifetime of CQDs from 3.46 → 3.79 ns without changing its tri-exponential functionality. Additionally, an increase in the concentration of AB (0.50 and 0.75 eq.) resulted in a slight decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs (3.76 → 3.71 ns), which was deemed negligible. This decrease may indicate the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex (CQDs + AB). The fluorescence decay profiles of CQDs and CQDs + AB complexes exhibited a tri-exponential function with minor variations in lifetime values (3.46 → 3.71 ns). These findings suggest that there is no significant alteration in fluorescence lifetime in the presence or absence of AB drugs. The formation of a ground state complex between CQDs and AB drugs was corroborated by the static quenching mechanism. Furthermore, the obtained fluorescence lifetime and the linear Stern-Volmer (S-V) plot confirmed that the quenching process is exclusively static.

The impact of pH on the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs upon binding with AB was investigated across varying pH levels. Acidic conditions were generated by preparing a solution with a pH range of 1 to 7 through the addition of 1.0 M H2SO4, while basic conditions (pH 7 to 13) were achieved using 1.0 M KOH. The fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs exhibited a linear increase at 433 nm (λex = 360 nm) under acidic conditions (Fig. 5 a), whereas a decrease was observed at 433 nm under basic conditions (Fig. 5b). Additionally, a color change from transparent to pale yellow was noted between pH 7 and 13. This alteration in color could be attributed to variations in electronic transitions, such as π → π* and n → π*, occurring in graphite nanodomains when their valence bands are filled or depleted, respectively [50]. Therefore, it can be inferred that the fluctuations in fluorescence intensity and color under different pH conditions are linked to distinct functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs. These findings suggest that when CQDs interact with AB at different pH levels, they exhibit distinct emission characteristics, rendering them promising candidates for various biological and sensing applications.

Upon the addition of AB at a concentration of 100 μM, CQDs exhibited an immediate fluorescence quenching at 433 nm, requiring less than 0 min to achieve over 90 % of their maximum fluorescence emission intensity (λem = 433 nm) (Figure SI4). Slight variations in the initial fluorescence emission intensity were observed after 0 min, with minimal changes observed as the reaction time extended up to 30 min. This rapid response suggests that the sensing of AB by CQDs is exceedingly fast and marginally less stable, as evidenced by the slight decrease in the intensity of the emission peak. This observation hints at the possibility of morphological changes in CQDs, leading to the formation of agglomerate structures, a phenomenon corroborated by HRTEM analysis [51].

To ensure robust binding of CQDs with AB, an interference study was conducted with other respiratory drugs (TP, SB, LSB, and LB), biomolecules (Uric acid [UA], Ascorbic acid [AA], and Dopamine [DA]), and naturally occurring metal ions (Ca, Mg, and Na) in the presence of CQDs + AB (1 μM). The addition of a 100-fold higher concentration (100 μM) of the aforementioned interferons to CQDs + AB (1.0 μM) solution did not significantly alter the fluorescence intensity of CQDs + AB (Fig. 6 ) [52]. This outcome indicates that none of the aforementioned interferons impede the strong binding nature of the CQDs + AB complex, even at 100-fold higher concentrations. These interferons were unable to compete with AB, affirming the high selectivity of CQDs towards AB drugs.

To elucidate the structural and crystalline alterations in CQDs following AB sensing, XRD analysis was conducted. In pristine CQDs, characteristic XRD peaks were observed around 2θ values of 21° to 29° and 42°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes, respectively (Figure SI5b) [33]. Remarkably, upon AB sensing, the position of the CQD peaks remained unchanged, while additional peaks corresponding to AB were discerned. Specifically, peaks at 2θ values of 23° and 42° were attributed to CQDs, while additional peaks at 2θ values of 21°, 28°, 38°, and 45° were indicative of AB presence (Fig. 8 a). The XRD patterns obtained for CQDs + AB revealed peaks at 2θ values of 23° and 42°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of CQDs, respectively. The XRD pattern for pristine AB is depicted in Figure SI5a. These findings suggest the formation of a graphitic CQDs + AB complex.

Structural defects in CQDs post-AB sensing were further investigated using Micro Raman spectroscopy. Before AB sensing, the Raman spectra of CQDs exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to the D and G bands at 1340 and 1572 cm−1, respectively, along with C-H vibration at 1437 cm−1, consistent with graphene characteristics (Figure SI5c) [33]. The integrated intensity ratio (ID/IG) between the D and G bands for pure CQDs was approximately 0.85. Following AB sensing, a blue shift (∼7 cm−1) in both the D and G bands of CQDs was observed, with peaks shifting from 1340 to 1347 cm−1 (D band) and 1572 to 1579 cm−1 (G band), while the ID/IG intensity ratio remained unchanged at 0.85, indicative of the preservation of the graphitic nature (Fig. 8b). This shift implies alterations in sp3 defects and sp2 carbon bonds upon AB sensing. Additionally, a peak at 1103 cm−1 corresponding to theophylline of AB drug was discerned [54]. Notably, Raman spectroscopy confirmed the presence of surface defects, a moderate change in graphitic nature, and the formation of agglomerate structures. These results provide clear evidence of AB molecules attaching to surface functional groups of CQDs, thereby inducing agglomeration.

Electron microscopy techniques, including FESEM, HRTEM, and AFM, were employed to investigate the surface morphology of CQDs following AB sensing. Initially, FESEM analysis revealed the spherical morphology of pristine CQDs (Figure SI6(a-c)) [33]. However, upon AB sensing, notable morphological changes were observed, with the formation of agglomerated structures evident in Fig. 9 A (a-c). These observations depict the altered morphology of CQDs + AB, with an estimated average diameter of approximately 14 nm. This observation is consistent with HRTEM images depicted in Fig. 9B(a-f). Before AB sensing, CQDs exhibited a particle size of approximately 3 nm (Figure SI7), which increased to ∼ 14 nm post-AB sensing. Additionally, the obtained Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern provided further evidence of the amorphous nature of CQDs after AB sensing (Fig. 9B(e)), with only the (002) and (100) planes of CQDs detected, indicative of their amorphous nature. This SAED pattern aligns well with XRD findings, confirming the amorphous nature of CQDs + AB. Notably, the absence of lattice fringes suggests the formation of agglomerated morphology between CQDs + AB.

Furthermore, AFM analysis, including 2D and 3D micrographs, elucidated the surface roughness of bare CQDs, measured at 12.53 nm, indicating multiple layers in the graphene structure (Figure SI8). Following AB sensing, AFM analysis revealed agglomerated morphology (Fig. 10 (a-d)). Consequently, CQDs + AB exhibited a height profile of approximately 18 nm (Fig. 10d) and roughness of 2.5 nm, equivalent to a single layer of graphene [55]. These AFM findings provide insights into the deposition of layers in the graphitic structure based on roughness, further elucidating the morphological alterations induced by AB sensing.

The presence of functional groups on CQDs + AB was elucidated through FTIR in the spectral range of 400 to 4000 cm−1. Specifically, hydroxyl group (υOH) and carbonyl group (υC=O) peaks were identified at 3215 and 1625 cm−1, respectively, corresponding to both CQDs and AB drug (Figure SI9). Additionally, a less intense peak observed at 2337 cm−1, attributed to sp3 C–H bond vibration, along with a new band at 2116 cm−1, associated with carbonyl stretching vibration (υC=O) of the AB drug [56], further confirmed the presence of functional groups post-AB sensing. Furthermore, the disappearance of the peak at 1527 cm−1 from bare CQDs indicated agglomeration formation upon AB sensing.

The chemical composition and bonds of CQDs after AB sensing were investigated via XPS. Before AB sensing, XPS survey scans of bare CQDs exhibited peaks at 286, 400.9, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, with atomic percentages of 76.5, 2.5, and 21.0 % (Figure SI10). Post-AB sensing, XPS survey scans of CQDs + AB revealed additional peaks at 285.79, 400.1, 532.69, and 70.99 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Br 3d, with atomic percentages of 61.94, 4.76, 26.81, and 0.49 %, respectively (Fig. 11 a). High-resolution C1s spectra displayed peaks at 285.1 and 286.5 eV, attributed to sp2 hybrid carbon (C-C) and sp3 hybrid carbon (C-C/C-N) attached to the CQDs' surface [33]. Peaks at 288.3 and 289.4 eV represented C = O and O = C-O from AB, respectively (Fig. 11b) [57]. Additionally, O1s spectra exhibited peaks at 531.0 and 534.9 eV, corresponding to C-C = O and C = O groups, indicating oxygen functional groups on CQDs + AB surfaces (Fig. 11c). The N1s spectrum depicted peaks at 400.1 and 401.3 eV, representing carbon-bonded nitrogen (C-N) and (C = N) in CQDs + AB, suggesting attachment of the AB molecule's nitrogen group to CQDs surfaces (Fig. 11d) [58]. Moreover, Br 3d high-resolution XPS spectra displayed doublets at 71.5, 70.5, and 68.4 eV, indicative of 2(C-Br) and Br−, confirming the presence of Br in the AB structure (Fig. 11e) [59]. These XPS findings strongly corroborate the formation of CQDs + AB complex post-AB sensing.

Inflammation, often stemming from protein denaturation, stands as a significant factor in various pathological conditions, particularly certain rheumatic diseases where the resultant autoantigens exacerbate the inflammatory response [60]. Despite their effectiveness, conventional anti-inflammatory drugs, such as Diclofenac sodium, commonly entail adverse effects, including gastrointestinal discomfort [61]. Therefore, exploring novel anti-inflammatory agents with improved safety profiles is crucial. To evaluate the potential of CQDs-AB in inhibiting protein denaturation, BSA and Ly were employed as positive and negative controls, respectively. Comparative analyses were conducted with Diclofenac sodium to discern the efficacy of CQDs-AB. Remarkably, CQDs-AB demonstrated substantial inhibition of BSA and Ly denaturation, exhibiting inhibition rates of 88.86 %, 93.29 %, and 95.08 % for BSA, and 95.43 %, 95.23 %, and 96.47 % for Ly, at concentrations of 10, 30, and 50 µL, respectively (Fig. 12 a, b). Notably, the inhibitory effect on BSA denaturation surpassed that on Ly, underscoring the specificity of CQDs-AB. Intriguingly, CQDs-AB exhibited superior anti-inflammatory activity compared to Diclofenac sodium.

Fig. 12c schematically illustrates the binding of CQDs-AB to BSA. CQDs-AB are postulated to interact with BSA via electrostatic interactions or hydrogen bonding. BSA harbors four distinct binding sites: site 1, located within its central cavity, and sites 2 to 4, situated at the interface between adjacent subdomains. These sites, characterized by amino acid substitutions, facilitate inter-domain interactions within BSA. The binding pattern of CQDs-AB onto BSA is influenced by the solution pH. At pH 6.3, CQDs-AB exhibit binding to both the central cavity and the interface between neighboring subdomains, thereby promoting surface adsorption [62]. Importantly, the safety profile of the CQDs-AB used in this study warrants consideration for potential therapeutic applications in humans.

In this investigation, the toxicological impact of CQDs was evaluated on Zebrafish at varying concentrations (10, 20, 30, and 40 µM) across different time points (24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf). In vivo toxicity was assessed through the monitoring of morphological changes, mortality rates, and embryonic development in Zebrafish [63]. At 24 hpf, normal embryonic growth was evident across all doses, while pericardial edema manifested solely at the highest concentration (40 µM). Remarkably, no mortality was recorded at any CQD concentration (Fig. 13 a). Notably, numerous small black dots exhibiting blue fluorescence were discernible throughout the Zebrafish, concentrated within the organs and tissues of the embryonic fish.

Incubation of fish embryos with CQDs at various hpf stages (24 to 96 hpf) elicited no observable morphological alterations, indicating the safety of these solutions for fish embryos up to 96 hpf. Fig. 13b illustrates the notable blue fluorescence exhibited by fish embryos incubated with CQDs, underscoring the exceptional fluorescence emission properties of CQDs, even post-internalization by the fish embryo. The amalgamated images in Fig. 13b corroborate the absorption of CQDs by the abdomen, brain, and dorsum of the Zebrafish, thereby highlighting their potential utility as effective fluorescence tags, owing to their impressive blue fluorescence characteristics.

Collectively, our findings indicate the non-toxic nature of CQDs on fish embryos, even at elevated concentrations (40 µM), positioning them as promising candidates for diverse practical biological applications.

In conclusion, this study represents the inaugural investigation into the utility of CQDs for the ultra-sensitive determination of subnanomolar concentrations of AB via fluorescence techniques. Employing a multi-faceted analytical approach, the agglomerative morphological alterations of CQDs through AB sensing were substantiated through XRD, FTIR, FESEM, AFM, Raman spectroscopy, and XPS analyses. The HRTEM examinations validated the amorphous character of CQDs + AB, with an average particle size of 14 nm. The attained selectivity and remarkable sensitivity of CQDs towards AB were underscored through non-radiative energy transfer of a maximum of 64 % and the formation of a ground state complex, CQDs + AB, with a binding constant (Ka) of 1.39194 × 10-3 M−1 (R2 = 0.99089). The established limit of detection (0.142 nM) showcases the capability of detecting AB within subnanomolar concentration ranges across diverse biological systems. TCSPC analyses further validated the formation of the ground-state complex, facilitating static quenching and energy transfer between CQDs (donor) and AB (acceptor). Moreover, in vivo toxicity assessments on zebrafish revealed minimal adverse effects, with a tolerance rate of 99 % towards CQDs. Furthermore, in vitro evaluations of the anti-inflammatory activity of CQDs + AB against both positive and negative proteins demonstrated superior efficacy compared to Diclofenac sodium. These findings emphasize the immense potential of as-prepared CQDs, boasting a large Stokes-shift, for a myriad of biological applications, with promising prospects in the development of anti-inflammatory therapeutics in the future.
